coredata.dc:description: The emulsion gels transformed by Pickering emulsions have great potential as a novel platform for oral drug delivery. In this study, an innovative alginate-based fluorescent colloidal particle was synthesized by Ugi reaction with alginate and carbon dots, which can stabilize Pickering emulsion at the present of Ca2+. The Pickering emulsions were converted to emulsion gels during gastric digestion. The microstructure of interfacial film was characterized in situ with confocal laser scanning microscopy. In the process of simulated gastrointestinal digestion, phase conversion of the Pickering emulsion was observed directly in situ by an automatic living cell imaging system. The results showed that clear fluorescent interfacial films can be visualized after simulated oral digestion, and fluorescent spheres were appeared after gastrointestinal digestion, indicating that Pickering emulsions were transformed into emulsion gels. Moreover, results on cell viability and cell uptake showed that the Pickering emulsion had good biocompatibility, which could be taken up by Caco-2 cells and could sustain the release of the drug. The formation of emulsion gels delays the digestion rate and improves the stability of encapsulated curcumin (CUR). In particular, in situ visualization technique also has promising applications in the digestion process and cellular uptake.

head.ce:title: Ca2+/pH-triggered gelation of Pickering emulsion in vitro digestion: Visualization and sustained-release performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emulsion gels transformed by Pickering emulsions have great potential as a novel platform for oral drug delivery. In this study, an innovative alginate-based fluorescent colloidal particle was synthesized by Ugi reaction with alginate and carbon dots, which can stabilize Pickering emulsion at the present of Ca2+. The Pickering emulsions were converted to emulsion gels during gastric digestion. The microstructure of interfacial film was characterized in situ with confocal laser scanning microscopy. In the process of simulated gastrointestinal digestion, phase conversion of the Pickering emulsion was observed directly in situ by an automatic living cell imaging system. The results showed that clear fluorescent interfacial films can be visualized after simulated oral digestion, and fluorescent spheres were appeared after gastrointestinal digestion, indicating that Pickering emulsions were transformed into emulsion gels. Moreover, results on cell viability and cell uptake showed that the Pickering emulsion had good biocompatibility, which could be taken up by Caco-2 cells and could sustain the release of the drug. The formation of emulsion gels delays the digestion rate and improves the stability of encapsulated curcumin (CUR). In particular, in situ visualization technique also has promising applications in the digestion process and cellular uptake.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Haifang Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing-original draft. Riting Huang: Investigation. Xinyu Zhao: Investigation. Shujuan Yang: Resources, Investigation, Validation. Furui He: Review. Wenqi Qin: Investigation. Junhao Huang: Review, Validation. Gaobo Yu: Review, Validation. Yuhong Feng: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Jiacheng Li*: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Chenghong Liao*: Conceptualization, Supervision.

Curcumin (CUR) has been widely recognized as a health-promoting compound due to its significant anticancer, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antidiabetic (Ruiz, Martin, Cosero, Jimenez, & Londono, 2018; Chang, Meikle, Su, Wang, Dekiwadia, Drummond, et al., 2019; D'Angelo, Noronha, Kurnik, Camara, Vieira, Abrunhosa, et al., 2021; Goncalves et al., 2021). However, the limited stability and poor water solubility restrict its applications in foods and pharmaceuticals. To address these challenges, some Pickering emulsions stabilized by inorganic nanoparticles or polymeric colloids, such as silica (Tikekar, Pan, & Nitin, 2013), cellulose nanocrystal (Aw et al., 2022), starch (Feng, Hu, Wang, Zhu, Chen, Zhuang, et al., 2020), chitosan (Shah, Li, Jin, An, He, Li, et al., 2016), and alginate (Sookkasem, Chatpun, Yuenyongsawad, & Wiwattanapatapee, 2015), were used to encapsulate CUR (Liu, Liu, Wang, & Li, 2021). The Pickering emulsion stabilized by silica nanoparticle enhanced the stability of encapsulated CUR and sustained its releasing, though partial aggregation of emulsion droplets occurred after the digestion of simulated gastric fluid (SGF) (Tikekar et al., 2013). Bakht developed chitosan-tripolyphosphate (CS-TPP) nanoparticles through ionic gelation technique using CS and TPP to stabilize Pickering emulsion and encapsulate CUR, which showed high stability against pH and salts (Shah et al., 2016). However, most emulsions are extremely unstable in the gastrointestinal system due to their sensitivity to physiological pH, ionic strengths and enzymes, therefore they fail to protect CUR from the destruction of physiological environment before the encapsulated CUR reaches the target sites (Calahorra, Akhtar, & Sarkar, 2018). The Pickering emulsion gel as a class of colloidal material possessed the advantages of both hydrogels and emulsions (Li et al., 2020; Yang, Gong, et al., 2019), which built up with oil droplets entrapped in three-dimensional networks ( Mao, Lu, Cui, Miao, & Gao, 2020; Morales, Martinez, & Pilosof, 2019) and exhibited superior stabilization (physical and oxidative stability). Furthermore, gelation can delay the digestion of bioactive compounds that encapsulated in the way of delaying the diffusion of digestive enzymes into the gel matrix (Zhang et al., 2021). Interesting, the gelation of emulsions can be performed via modulating magnitude of triggering forces (Mao et al., 2020; Morales et al., 2019) (e.g., pH, temperature, salt, enzyme). Therefore, we hypothesized that achieving emulsion-to-gel conversion during the gastrointestinal digestion would contribute to enhance the stability and improve the delivery of the encapsulated CUR.

Emulsions stabilized by polysaccharide are widely used in encapsulation, controlled release, and targeted delivery of bioactive food ingredients due to its excellent properties of non-toxicity, easy digested, biocompatible and stability (Zhang et al., 2020). The stabilization mechanisms mainly include the interface membrane, network structure in the continuous phase, and depletion interactions (Cui et al., 2021). Alginate (ALG) is a kind of versatile polysaccharide that extracted from brown algae and bacterial sources. Due to its outstanding advantages, such as good biodegradability, biocompatibility, and modifiability, ALG has been used in numerous applications (Fernando, Lee, Han, & Ahn, 2020). Nevertheless, ALG is too hydrophilic to adsorb on the interfaces, which is required to be modified as an emulsifier (Nan et al., 2014). Hence, ALG can be designed as an amphipathic colloidal particle to absorb at the oil-water interface and stabilize Pickering emulsion by introducing hydrophobic chains onto its hydrophilic backbone (Lee & Mooney, 2012). Previous studies have shown that the effect of molecular weight and pH on the self-assembly, microstructure and emulsification of amphiphilic ALG colloidal particles (Fang, Zhao, Yu, Zhang, Feng, Zhou, et al., 2020). In addition, the gelation mechanism of ALG is that sodium ions in ALG molecules are replaced by Ca2+ or H+, thus forming a Ca2+-cross-linked or hydrogen bond-linked network structure (Lin, Kelly, & Miao, 2021b). It's also demonstrated that ALG can form stronger hydrogel networks under gastric and become weaker in the intestinal environment due to its pH sensitivity (Mascaraque, Sanz, Hogan, Rubio, & Brodkorb, 2019). However, the phase conversion of Pickering emulsion during digestion is a challenging task. Therefore, we speculated that ALG based emulsion may form the emulsion gel during gastrointestinal digestion.

During the digestion process, the complex digestion microenvironment (e.g., pH, droplet size, lipases, proteases, other enzymes and cofactors) affects the microstructure of interfacial film and ultimately determines the fate of the Pickering emulsion (Cerpnjak, Zvonar, Gasperlin, & Vrecer, 2013; Sarkar, Zhang, Holmes, & Ettelaie, 2019), and influences the bioavailability of CUR. However, a universal relationship between interfacial behavior of Pickering emulsion and emulsion digestion remains to be established. The correlation promotes the understanding of the influence of gastrointestinal microenvironment on the microstructure of interfacial film, providing a theoretical basis and technical approach for predicting and controlling the release of drugs by regulating the phase transition of the emulsion (Lu, Mao, Hou, Miao, & Gao, 2019). Herein, it is of great significance to develop a visualization technique to explore the impact of the complex interfaces composed by particles, biopolymers, and ion on the fate of Pickering emulsions once ingested. Up to now, the direct visualization of the Pickering emulsions interfacial film that tightly packed with colloidal particles may be achieved by the introducing of fluorophores characteristic biopolymers. It could not only increase their interparticle interactions and aggregation properties in solution, but also enhance droplet-droplet interactions while adsorbed at the oil-water interface (Huang, Na, Xiong, Wang, & Peng, 2016; Sabri et al., 2019). In previous studies, we fabricated an amphiphilic ALG based fluorescent colloidal particle (Lin et al., 2021), which presented the potential of both stabilize Pickering emulsion and in situ visualize the Pickering emulsion interfacial film (Lin, Zhao, Yang, He, Qin, Gong et al., 2021; Wan, Zeng, Yang, Zhao, Wen, Tang, et al., 2022). Recently, carbon dots (CDs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be used for biological imaging by virtue of its remarkable properties, such as photoluminescence (PL) properties, biocompatibility (Li et al., 2021; Liu, Mo, Chen, Chen, Wu, Tang, et al., 2022; Xue et al., 2019). Interestingly, one kind hydrophobic CDs with blue dispersed emission and red aggregation-induced emission were successfully obtained through a one-pot solvothermal treatment (Yang, Liu, Guo, Lei, Zhuang, Zhang, et al., 2019). Therefore, grafting CDs will be advantageous for the direct visualization of the Pickering emulsion.

In our previous work, ALG was grafted on the surface of silica nanoparticles and used for emulsion drug delivery, which showed pH responsiveness derived from its abundant carboxyl groups along the molecular backbone as well as high stable due to the formation of three-dimensional network (Chen et al., 2017). Hence, the hypothesis of this research was that ALG grafted with CDs nanoparticles (named ALG-CDs) could achieve the purpose of emulsion stabilization, drug encapsulation and bioimaging. In addition, ALG-CDs stabilized Pickering emulsion would transform to emulsion gel in the intestinal phase and visualize in situ. We aimed to investigate the gastrointestinal digestion properties of Pickering emulsion and assess the microstructural fate of the Pickering emulsion during in simulated gastrointestinal digestion as well as the release of Free fatty acid (FFA) in the intestinal phase. Furthermore, we demonstrated the potential use of Pickering emulsion on encapsulation model bioactive compound, curcumin (CUR), which could be designed to delay the rate of hydrophobic bioactivities release in vitro digestion. Finally, the biocompatibility evaluation and cellular imaging were fully performed. This study therefore recommends that the Pickering emulsion stabilized by fluorescent colloidal particle ALG-CDs can be potentially applied in situ visualization for in vitro digestion, controllable delivery system for hydrophobic nutraceuticals, and tracer of cellular uptake.

Sodium alginate (ALG, M w = 621,814 and M n = 416,963), cyclohexyl isonitrile, formaldehyde (AR), sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, ethanol, anhydrous calcium chloride, acetic acid (anhydrous), dithio-salicylic acid (DTSA), curcumin (≥95%), Diemthyl Sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Melamine (MA) was ordered from Macklin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Artificial saliva and pepsin from porcine mucosa were obtained from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Porcine bile extract and porcine lipase were purchased from Bomei Bio-technology CO. Ltd. (Hefei, China). Food-grade Fook Linmen peanut oil was supplied by Cofco Group Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China) and used as the oil phase. For cell culture experiments, human Caco-2 cells were purchased from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). DMEM and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were supplied by BI (Biological Industries, Israel). Trypsin, phosphate buffered Saline (PBS) and penicillin-streptomycin mixture (5000 U/ml) were obtained from Gibco Cell Culture Products (Thermo Fisher Scientiﬁc, US). CCK-8 kit was obtained from Dojindo (Japan). Each reagent utilized in this study was analytically pure and were used without further purification. Milli-Q water (resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C) was used in all experiments.

ALG-CDs is synthesized through Ugi reaction according to previous work by our research group with some modifications (Yan et al., 2016). Ugi reaction is a four-component condensation reaction of sodium alginate with formaldehyde, cyclohexyl isonitrile, and hydrophobic amino carbon dots (H-CDs). Before synthesis of ALG-CDs, the H-CDs were prepared through a one-pot solvothermal process with melamine (MA), dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) and acetic acid solution (Yang, Gong, et al., 2019). Then, 1 g of sodium alginate was dissolved in 40 ml distilled water for stirring 12 h at room temperature and the pH was adjusted to 3.6 using HCl solution (0.5 mol/L). Next, 0.146 mL of formaldehyde, 0.24 g of H-CDs dissolved in DMSO (3 mL), and 0.125 mL of cyclohexyl isonitrile were successively added to the solution, stirring at 35 °C for 24 h under dark environment. At last, the resultant products were dialyzed against distilled water for 5 days to remove the ingredient of unreacted and then lyophilized to obtain the pure product (ALG-CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pickering emulsion by ALG-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before emulsification, different concentrations of Ca2+ (0, 0.004, 0.01, 0.04, 0.08 M) were added to the stock solution of ALG-CDs as continuous phase (volume ratio = 1:9). Peanut oil was added as dispersion phase. The Pickering emulsion was prepared by mixing ALG-CDs solution and peanut oil at 22,000 rpm for 8 min using a high shear dispersion homogenizer (FA25, FLUKOF, Germany) at room temperature (volume ratio = 10:10). The Pickering emulsion type was determined by observing the result of adding a drop emulsion to a volume of either pure oil or water (drop test).

The FT-IR spectra were taken on a Bruker Tensor 27 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The samples were mixed with KBr and pressed to semitransparent wafers and scanned in the 400-4000 cm−1 region. 1H NMR was performed on a Bruker nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (AV400, Switzerland) at 75 °C, the samples were dissolved in D2O to 10 mg/mL in a 5 mm NMR tube. To examine the degree of substitution (DS) of ALG-CDs, the integral peak area (S1) of characteristic H-1 proton of CDs and the integral peak area (S2) of alginate protons were determined. The formula for DS calculation is as follows (Chiang & Chu, 2015). The surface tension of the ALG solutions before and after modification was measured under 25 °C using the Wilhelmy plate method by a KRUSS automatic surface tensiometer (K100, Germany). The glass was coated with paraffin and the samples’ contact angle was determined using an A-300 optical tensiometer (Ningbo, China).

The morphology of ALG-CDs colloidal particles in distilled water was observed by JEOL transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100, Japan). During the sample preparation, the diluted aggregates solutions were dropped on a copper grid and stained with 2% phosphotungstic acid solution, and then dried by an infrared lamp. Fluorescence spectra of ALG-CDs micellar aggregates in aqueous solution were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lambda 750s). The morphology of Pickering emulsion stabilized by the ALG-CDs@Ca2+ was observed using an optical microscopy (Nikon Eclipse E200, Nikon, Japan). The average droplet size of freshly prepared emulsions was estimated by Nano Measurer 1.2 software. The stabilization of Pickering emulsions was measured by Turbiscan Lab Expert stability analyzer (Formulaction, France) at 25 °C. The surface morphology of Pickering emulsions droplets polymerized by styrene (used as the replaced oil phase, containing 2 mol% AIBN as initiator) at 60 °C for 24 h was observed by SEM (Phenom, FEI, Netherlands). The emulsions with different Ca2+ were placed into cylindrical glass tubes and scanned 100 times for 3 h. CLSM spectrometer (Olympus FV1000, Japan) was used to observe the morphology of solid ALG-CDs and the microstructure of Pickering emulsion with different concentrations of Ca2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulated digestion of pickering emulsion in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro simulated digestion of emulsions was evaluated by a three-stage static gastrointestinal tract (GIT) model, which included oral/mouth, stomach and small intestine stages (Mao & McClements, 2012). ALG-CDs@Ca2+ stabilized Pickering emulsions were sequentially digested by stock solutions of simulated saliva fluid (SSF), simulated gastric fluid (SGF), and simulated intestinal fluid (SIF) according to previous studies with minor modification (Marefati, Bertrand, Sjöö, Dejmek, & Rayner, 2017; McClements & Li, 2010; Wei, Wang, Liu, Mackie, Zhang, Dai, et al., 2022).

Firstly, 5 mL SSF (pH 6.8) was added to 50 mL conical flask and balanced in an incubator shaker at 37 °C for 5 min. Then 5 mL Pickering emulsions were mixed with 5 mL SSF and swirled continuously at 100 rpm in the incubator at 37 °C for 5 min to mimic oral conditions.

SGF was prepared by dissolving NaCl (2 g) in distilled water and the pH was adjusted to 2.5 with HCl (7 mL). Porcine pepsin (3.2 g) was dispersed in SGF with the total volume reaching up to 1 L. The samples from the oral phase (10 mL) were mixed with SGF (10 mL) that containing pepsin (0.0032 g/L) in 50 mL conical flask. Finally, the mixture was swirled continuously at 100 rpm in the incubator at 37 °C for 1 h to mimic the conditions in the stomach.

SIF was composed of pancreatic lipase (4.8 mg/mL), bile extract solution (5 mg/mL) and calcium chloride solution (750 mM). Samples obtained from the simulated gastric digestion were mixed with SIF (volume ratio = 20:20) in 50 mL conical flask and shook at 100 rpm in the incubator at 37 °C for 2 h to mimic the conditions in the intestinal conditions. Meanwhile, the pH of samples was maintained at 7.0 using NaOH (0.25 M) in the whole intestinal stage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fate of pickering emulsion in vitro digestion
ce:section.ce:para: Etaluma LS720 automatic living cell imaging system (ALCIS, Lumascopes, America) was used to visualize the microstructure of the emulsion at different digestion stages. Initially, the digesta sample were collected at the end of each digestion stage that including oral phase, gastric phase, intestinal digestion for 1 h, and intestinal digestion for 2 h. Then an aliquot of the samples was placed on a microscope slide and covered with a cover slip. The morphology of digesta was observed at the excitation sources of 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free fatty acids (FFAs) releasing of pickering emulsion in the small intestine stage
ce:section.ce:para:
The release of free fatty acids (FFAs) was determined using pH-stat protocol (Chen et al., 2022; Hur, Lee, & Lee, 2015). After gastric digestion, the digesta were mixed with SIF and the pH of mixtures was re-adjusted to 7.0. During the simulated intestinal stage, the automatic titration unit was used to monitor and maintain the pH at 7.0 by adding 0.25 M NaOH solution into the mixture. The consumption volume of NaOH was recorded to calculate the percentage of FFAs released using the following formula:
% FFA =
V
NaOH ×
m
NaOH ×
M lipid
2 ×
W lipid
× 100
Where V NaOH (mL) is the volume of NaOH used to neutralize the FFAs produced, m NaOH is the molarity of NaOH (0.25 M), M lipid is the weight of lipid initially present in the reaction flask (884 g/mol), W lipid is the molecular weight of the oil.

In order to investigate the potential application of Pickering emulsions stabilized by ALG-CDs@Ca2+ in drug delivery systems, curcumin (CUR) was encapsulated on the Pickering emulsion. Initially, 10 mg of CUR was dispersed in 10 mL of peanut oil at room temperature for 1 h. Then 1 mL of the obtained CUR-loaded peanut oil mixed with 9 mL of blank peanut oil, which was used as dispersion phase. The continuous phase was also ALG-CDs (1 mg/mL) solution with different Ca2+. Finally, the CUR-loaded emulsion was prepared by mixing dispersed phase and continuous phase at 22,000 rpm for 8 min using a high shear dispersion homogenizer at room temperature (volume ratio = 10:10).

After the CUR-loaded Pickering emulsions were prepared, 0.1 mL of the emulsion was added to 0.9 mL of methanol with vortex-mixing for 1 min respectively. Then 0.3 mL of the mixture was added to 0.7 mL of methanol with sufficient mixing for 1 min and centrifuged at 15,000 g for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected and passed through a 0.20 μm syringe filter. The pre-treatment of CUR standard was the same as the samples. The filter liquid was detected by HPLC system. UV detection was used at 250 nm. The mobile phase was 70% methanol and 30% distill water with the column temperature fixed at 35 °C. The encapsulation efficiency (EE) of CUR in Pickering emulsion was calculated as follows:
Encapsulation
Efficiency
( % )
=
M e
M add
× 100
Here, M add was the initial-added amont of CUR in the oil phase, M e was the amont of CUR in the emulsion.

As mentioned above, the Pickering emulsion stabilized by ALG-CDs@Ca2+ loaded CUR. CUR could be easily affected by many environmental factors during the in vitro simulated digestion process, such as pH, light, heat, ions and so on, which were likely to cause its loss. Therefore, it's necessary to calculate the retained CUR after each digestion stage. The extraction and detection procedures for CUR were the same as above process, except that the mixture sourced from each digestion stage. The retained rate of CUR after each digestion stage was calculated as follows:
Retention
rate
of
Cur =
C digesta
C initial
× 100
Where C digesta was the total CUR content retained after each digestion stages, C initial was the initial CUR content in Pickering emulsions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cytotoxicity study of CUR containing pickering emulsion
ce:section.ce:para:
CaCo-2 cell model is the most common cell in predicting drug absorption in human small intestine and investigating the transport mechanism of drug. Cytotoxic effect of the blank Pickering emulsion (ALG-CDs@Ca2+ (0.04 M) stabilized Pickering emulsion, ACPE), CUR-loaded Pickering emulsion (ALG-CDs@Ca2+ (0.04 M) stabilized Pickering emulsion encapsulated CUR, ACPEC), and free CUR (FC) on Caco-2 cells were evaluated after 24 h and 48 h treatment using cell counting kit 8 (CCK-8) assay. Caco-2 cells were cultured with Dulbecco's modiﬁed Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin mixture. The cells were incubated at 37 °C in a humidiﬁed incubator with 5% CO2, and detached from the Petri dish with trypsin. Caco-2 cells were seeded at a density of 1.0 × 104 cells/100 mL in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h. Then ALG-CDs@Ca2+ (0.04 M) stabilized Pickering emulsions loading CUR and pure CUR were added with the final concentration of CUR reached up to 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 μg/mL. Simultaneously, ALG-CDs@Ca2+ (0.04 M) stabilized Pickering emulsions were added as the blank emulsion with the same proportion of CUR-loaded Pickering emulsion (0, 1%, 2%, 3%, 4%, 5%). The cells were incubated with samples for 24 h and 48 h, respectively and washed thrice with PBS. Afterwards, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was mixed with 100 μL complete DMEM medium and added to each well. After 2 h of incubation, the optical density (OD) of each well was measured using a microplate reader measured at Bio-Tek microplate reader (America) at a wavelength of 450 nm. Cell survival was calculated as follows:
Cell
survival
rate
( % )
=
A s
−
A 0
A c
−
A 0
× 100
Where, A s is the absorbance of the medium (DMEM medium, CCK-8 solution and cell) with sample; A 0 is the absorbance of the blank (DMEM medium and CCK-8 solution without cell); A c is the absorbance of the control group (medium without sample).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake of CUR containing pickering emulsion
ce:section.ce:para: The cell uptake ability of blank Pickering emulsion and CUR-loaded Pickering emulsion was investigated by Etaluma LS720 automatic living cell imaging system (ALCIS, Lumascopes, America). The concentration of CUR-loaded Pickering emulsion that added to co-culture with Caco-2 cells was 15 μg/mL, and the addition of blank Pickering emulsion was the same as CUR-loaded Pickering emulsion. Initially, Caco-2 cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 103 cells per 6 cm Petri dish with complete DMEM medium for 24 h. Then samples were added to complete DMEM medium. After incubation for 6 h, the medium was removed and the cells were washed with PBS for five times. At last, the cells were observed by ALCIS with the excitation source were 405 and 488 nm.

All assays were measured independently in triplicate per sample, and all data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The signiﬁcant of mean diﬀerences between two group were determined by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Dunnett's test. The value of p < 0.05 and p < 0.001 was considered as statistically signiﬁcant.

Hydrophobic amino Carbon Dots (H-CDs) were synthesized according to the methods described in the reference (Yang, Gong, et al., 2019). The reactants were milky white and the products were cardinal red, which turned into pink liquid under 365 nm UV irradiation, as seen in Fig. S1a. The products were rotated and dispersed in 500 mL distilled water, centrifuged for 10 min at 15,000 rpm, then freeze-dried to obtain pink power, which displayed red under 365 nm UV irradiation. The appearance of the obtained CDs was consistent with the literature (Yang, Gong, et al., 2019). FT-IR spectra and NMR were taken to further analyze the chemical structure of the H-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum uncovered the surface of the H-CDs, and the peaks were basically consistent with those in the literature. As there were no free hydroxy groups in the reactants, the broad peak at 3443 cm−1 could be assigned to –NH2 stretching vibration, and the peak at 1570 cm−1 can be also attributed to –NH2 stretching vibration (see Fig. S2). Furthermore, 1H NMR spectra were employed to speculate the hydrogen on the carbon skeleton. The multiple proton peaks with a chemical shift between 7.2 and 8.1 ppm in Fig. S4 can be attributed to the graphitized cores proton resonances. These 1H NMR results confirmed the successful synthesis of H-CDs.

In this research, amphiphilic ALG-CDs were synthesized by grafting hydrophobic amino CDs on ALG via Ugi four-component condensation reaction with the result showed in Fig. 1 A. 1H NMR spectrums of ALG and ALG-CDs were showed in Fig. 1B. The proton peaks of natural sodium alginate chain clearly presented at the range of δ3.5 to 5.5 (Fang et al., 2020). Compared with ALG, the proton peaks of ALG-CDs at δ1.2–1.8 derived from the proton of cyclohexyl group (Chen et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2016), the proton peak at δ2.5 derived from the amino group on the surface of carbon dots, which also indicated that ALG-CDs were successfully synthesized. The degree of substitution (DS) of CDs grafted to ALG was 10.20% and the reaction yield was 79.64%. Fig. 1C showed the FT-IR spectra of ALG and ALG-CDs. For the spectra of ALG, the broad peak at 3443 cm−1 was assigned to O–H stretching vibration. The adsorption band at 2926 cm−1 was due to methylene (–CH2) stretching vibration on the alginate skeleton (Yan et al., 2016). The characteristic peaks located at 1614 cm−1 and 1414 cm−1 were attributed to the –COO– asymmetric and symmetric stretching absorption peaks of ALG. The peak appearing at 1033 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration peak of C–O–C. ALG-CDs was modified and grafted with CDs, and the additional peaks were presented at 1736 cm−1 and 1264 cm−1, which were attributed to the –CO and aromatic C–NH stretching vibration of the amide bond in ALG-CDs, respectively.

From Table S1, the surface tension of ALG and ALG-CDs reached equilibrium at 53.6 mN/m, 46.28 mN/m. In Fig. S5, the contact angle of water was 114°, which was 108° and 118° for ALG and ALG-CDs, respectively. ALG contains large amounts of hydroxyl group and its molecule chains are difficult to migrate from the water to gas-liquid interface (Wen, Huang, Tang, He, Yuan, Wan, et al., 2022), and the carbon dots are hydrophobic. Therefore, the surface tension of ALG-CDs was lower than ALG and the contact angle of ALG-CDs was higher than ALG.

Due to the favorable fluorescence of H-CDs, we hypothesized ALG-CDs with excellent fluorescence by grafting ALG with H-CDs. The UV–Vis absorption and the fluorescence emission of as prepared ALG-CDs solid and solution were examined to evaluate their optical properties. As presented in Fig. S1c, the ALG-CDs solid showed a pale pinkish white at natural light and turned to pink under 365 nm UV irradiation. Moreover, the UV spectrum of ALG-CDs solution had an absorption peak at 348 nm, but ALG solution had no absorption peak (Fig. 2 B). Here, the molecular fluorescence spectra of ALG-CDs at different concentrations in aqueous solution was presented in Fig. 2C. The fluorescence intensity increased with the rasing of ALG-CDs concentration that was attributed to the aggregation-induced emission of H-CDs. The photographs in Fig. 2A (a-c) were the ALG-CDs solid sample excited under different excitation sources including 405, 488, and 546 nm, which demonstrate blue, green, and red fluorescent images, respectively. Fig. 2A (d) was the merged images of ALG-CDs by three channels. The above results showed that ALG-CDs was successfully synthesized with remarkable fluorescence, which can be used to achieve accurate labeling and localization (Alaghmandfard, Sedighi, Rezaei, Abedini, Khachatourian, Toprak et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of ALG-CDs aggregates in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: In this research, the extremely hydrophilic ALG grafted with H-CDs to synthesize amphiphilic ALG derivaties, which can arrange themselves into organized molecular assemblies in aqueous solution, generally, named micelles. The critical aggregate concentration (CAC) of ALG-CDs aggregates in aqueous solution was measured by fluorescence intensity corresponding to different concentration of ALG-CDs micelle solutions (Fig. 3 C). The inflection point of the fluorescence intensity confirmed that it would self-assemble and bind into micelles, so the CAC value was 0.083 mg/mL. In addition, the spherical micelles of ALG-CDs were observed in aqueous solution by the TEM measurement (Fig. 3B). The average size of the micelles was 84.05 ± 40 nm that was calculated by Nanomeasure software, and the size had a relative narrow distribution (Fig. 3A). The formation mechanism of spherical micelles was that H-CDs acted as lipophilic tails stretching toward the center and ALG acted as hydrophilic heads extending toward water. However, the micelles were molceular assemblies in constant movement with exchange of members occurring not only between micelles but also between the bulk aqueous solution and the micelle (Furton & Norelus, 1993).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and microstructure of ALG-CDs @ Ca2+ stabilized pickering emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the obtained ALG-CDs was self-assembled into colloidal particles in aqueous solution, and the colloidal particle was adsorbed on the oil-water interface. It prevented the aggregation and collision of droplets and stabilize the emulsion. The Turbiscan Lab Expert stability analyzer is nondestructive to samples. TSI (Turbiscan Stability Index, TSI) is a parameter that used to evaluate the stability of emulsion systems, and its value may be any number between 0 and 100. The lower the value, the more stable it is (Wiśniewska, Chibowski, & Urban, 2015; Zhao, Yu, Li, Feng, Zhang, Peng, et al., 2018). In order to further understand the stability of ALG-CDs with different concentration of Ca2+ assembled emulsions, the emulsification state and TSI were investigated in Fig. 4 (A-B). Although ALG-CDs alone can also stabilize Pickering emulsion, the emulsification height increase with the increased of Ca2+ concentration (0.004, 0.01, 0.04 M). The trend of TSI with Ca2+ concentration was consistent with the change in macroscopic stability of emulsions. Fig. 4C showed the optical microscope and particle size distribution of Pickering emulsion stabilized by ALG-CDs@Ca2+ (0, 0.004, 0.04, 0.08 M). The emulsion particle size trended toward smaller and the distribution trended toward more uniform with increasing of Ca2+. However, when the concentration of Ca2+ reached up to 0.08 M, a little oil phase precipitated gradually on the top of liquid level (Fig. 4A), and the droplet size became larger and unevenness, appearing the phenomenon of demulsification (Fig. 4C (d)). The microstructure of the Pickering emulsion can be clearly seen from CLSM images as shown in Fig. 4D. The oil droplets were encircled by bright green fluorescent rings (Fig. 4D (a-d)), confirming that ALG-CDs micelles with fluorescence characteristic formed interface-film around the droplets (Lin et al., 2021). What's more, the variation of droplet size and interfacial film thickness (Fig. 4D (a1-d1)) with Ca2+ concentration of the Pickering emulsion presented by CLSM is consistent with TSI and optical microscopy.

From the above results, the fluorescent ALG-CDs micelles aggregated at the oil-water interface to stabilize Pickering emulsion, macro and micro results were consistent and jointly proved that the increase of moderate Ca2+ would contribute to the stability of Pickering emulsion, but excess Ca2+ were detrimental to the stability of the emulsion. The reason must be that the appropriate amount of Ca2+ makes more colloidal particles migrate to the oil-water interface, the interfacial tension increases, the droplet becomes smaller, and the emulsion becomes more stable (Lin, Kelly, & Miao, 2021a; Lin et al., 2021; Zou, Guo, Yin, Wang, & Yang, 2015). What's more, the microstructure of amphiphilic ALG-CDs absorbing at the oil/water interface by SEM provided a better insight into the influence of excessive Ca2+ on emulsion structure (Fig. S6). The colloidal particles aggregation was due to the interaction between neighboring particles and Ca2+, which was likely to cause the unevenly covered of particles emulsifier. Interesting, abundant of spheres of ALG-CDs were aggregated at the oil-water interface at the concentration of Ca2+ was 0.04 M, but the spheres became less when the Ca2+ increased to 0.08 M. These phenomena were further confirmed that excessive Ca2+ would take colloidal particles away from the droplet surface by ionic cross-linking (Fig. S6), resulting in flocculation of colloidal particles and unable to absorb at the oil-water interface (Lin et al., 2021; Zhu, Zhao, & Gong, 2015), and the emulsion was destabilized (Fig. 4Dd). Herein, Ca2+ has the potential to adjust the stability of Pickering emulsion. Moreover, the emergence and development of in situ visualization techniques have opened up new directions for investigating the interfacial properties of emulsion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fate of pickering emulsion during simulate GIT digestion
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the digest phenomenon of the Pickering emulsion, the morphology of the Pickering emulsion stabilized by ALG-CDs with different Ca2+ in vitro digestion was observed by ALCIS under excitation sources 405 nm. In Fig. 5 A (a1-d1), clear interfacial membranes could be seen after simulated oral digestion, which indicated that the Pickering emulsions maintained their original shape. The morphology of Pickering emulsion without Ca2+ (0 M) and with excessive Ca2+ (0.08 M) changed from round to ellipse shape. However, the morphology of the Pickering emulsion was nearly no change with addition of moderate Ca2+ (0.004 and 0.04 M). The most likely reason is that the Pickering emulsion stabilized by ALG-CDs@Ca2+ (0 M, 0.08 M) was diluted by artificial saliva, resulting in the demulsification of unstable droplets, or even the coalescence of two or more emulsion droplets, and in turn fused into one large irregular droplet. The emulsion with moderate Ca2+ had relatively stable interface film, so the morphology was basically not affected. As can be seen from Fig. 5A (a2-d2), the fluorescent interface-film disappeared and the whole droplets became balls with blue fluorescence in SGF for 1 h, which indicated the formation of emulsion gels. The mechanisms of gelation for Pickering emulsion during gastric phase are follows. First, ALG can form acid gels at pH below pKa of the uronic acid (Bu, Kjøniksen, & Nyström, 2005) and the pH of simulated gastric liquids was maintained at 2.5, therefore, the Pickering emulsions were transformed into emulsion gels by proton-triggered. Second, the formation of ionic interaction that Ca2+ cross-linked with ALG was another contribution for the gelation of emulsion. From Fig. 5A (a2), it showed that the droplets were independent of each other and had no bridging behaviors, indicating that there was no cross-linking of ionic bonds between droplets in the absence of Ca2+. However, with the increase of Ca2+, the droplets showed hand in hand, this was probably because the ionic cross-linked interaction between droplets became stronger.

In fact, the gel structure of the Pickering emulsion after gastric digestion plays an important role in the diffusion of lipase, which in turn affects lipid digestion (Lu et al., 2019). As seen in Fig. 5A (a3-d3) and Fig. 5A (a4-d4), the size of droplets became smaller with the addition of Ca2+ (0, 0.004 and 0.04 M) and the extending of digestion time, suggesting that the droplets had been broken, which was due to the increased repulsion between ALG monomers at high pH (Chuang et al., 2017). What's more, there was an ionic exchange between Ca2+ in emulsion gels and Na+ in the simulated intestinal fluid (Lin, Kelly, & Miao, 2022). Of note, ALG-CDs is a kind of colloidal particle, which are more flexible and capable to adjust their morphology at the oil-water interface. In addition, there is electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged colloidal particles and negatively charged bile salts, making it difficult for these particles to be replaced by bile salts (Sarkar, Horne, & Singh, 2010; Wei et al., 2022). Therefore, the contact area for bile salts and lipase on the droplet surface was reduced and the lipolysis was inhibited, displaying irregular fluorescent droplets. When the added Ca2+ was 0.08 M, the droplets decreased but still bigger than other group, and the reason may be that Ca2+ in droplets were more than Na+ in intestinal fluid, so there were still some network structures formed by fluorescent colloidal particles surrounding the oil droplets in the continuous phase. Those results also indicated that the variable structure of ALG-CDs@Ca2+ stabilized Pickering emulsions was beneficial to delay release of drug or nutrient substance.

All the results indicated that the Pickering emulsion were converted into emulsion gels after gastric digestion by the combined effect of hydrogen bonding and Ca2+ cross-linking, which could be attributed to the three-dimensional network formed by alginate-based fluorescent colloidal particles in the continuous phase around the oil droplets. Previously, the polysaccharide-stabilized emulsions as delivery systems were vulnerable to the strongly acidic environment of gastric juice. Our ALG-CDs stabilized emulsion could not only visualize the structural changes during emulsion digestion in situ, but also transformed into gels, which had a more compact structure and stronger physical barrier. It supplied an optimistic application prospect in the delivery of nutrients and drugs, as well as in situ visualization the emulsion digestion.

Lipid digestion is one kind of bio-interfacial process which is largely controlled by the binding of lipase-colipase-bile salts complex onto the surface of emulsified lipid droplets (Sarkar et al., 2019). FFAs represent the speed of lipid digestion in SIF phase. Fig. 6 A showed FFAs release profiles for Pickering emulsions with different Ca2+ concentration, which is below 10% for cumulative release amount of FFAs at the end. The release of FFAs was mainly happened in the first 15 min, and then gradually slowed down. Compared with the addition of Ca2+, Pickering emulsion without Ca2+ had higher release rate of FFAs, finally reaching up to 8.0%. It was also found that lipid digestion rate was reduced with the addition of Ca2+ concentration. Ca2+ cross-linked in the alginate that caused bile salts, phospholipids and digestive enzyme were difficult to penetrate through the cross-linked network of the interfacial film. The higher concentration of Ca2+ was, the thicker the interfacial film was, which inhibited the movement of bile salts and lipases to lipid drops and contributed to the low release of FFAs. However, when the concentration of Ca2+ was 0.08 M, the emulsion was unstable and the droplets aggregated into large droplets or even be broken, and some oil droplets were direct contact with the lipase-colipase complex during the intestinal digestion and were easily digested. Therefore, the FFAs release value of the group “A+0.08 M Ca2+” is higher than the group "A+0.04 M Ca2+ ".

The low release of FFAs indicated the inhibition of lipid digestion. The adsorption of bile salts on the droplet surface was considered as a prerequisite for lipid digestion (Hu, Wu, Wang, Lu, Gao, Xu, et al., 2021). Therefore, the low release FFAs could be explained as follows. First and foremost, the Pickering emulsion turned to emulsion gels in gastric digestion, providing a more compact network with smaller pores and the oil droplets were trapped in them, then hindering the adsorption of bile salts (Li, Meng, et al., 2021). Second, the electrostatic interaction between bile salts and particles restrained the adsorption of bile salts on the droplet surface (Wei et al., 2022). Herein, the microstructure of Pickering emulsion changed the diffusion behavior of digestive enzymes (Chen et al., 2022), thereby inhibiting the hydrolysis of lipid droplets by lipase. Therefore, the digestive enzyme is another key factor affecting lipid digestion. All these inhibited the substitution of colloidal particles by bile salts and reduced the contact of lipase-colipase complexes with oil phase, thereby delaying the lipid digestion and FFA release. To sum up, lipids digestion for Pickering emulsions stabilized by ALG-CDs with different concentration of Ca2+ was inhibited, which could control and prolong intestinal release of nutraceuticals.

In Fig. 6B, the effect of Ca2+ on the EE of CUR during emulsification was also investigated. We measured and calculated the concentration of CUR in the inner water phase and in the collected emulsion by HPLC. EE of CUR was 75.25% in Pickering emulsion without adding Ca2+, which showed an upward trend with the increase of Ca2+ concentration. The highest EE was up to 97.07% when the concentration of Ca2+ reached to 0.04 M. The reason was that Ca2+ cross-linking in the alginate led to more ALG-CDs accumulating at the oil-water interface, which could encapsulate more CUR. However, EE was decreased to 86.55% when the Ca2+ concentration was 0.08 M. The reason could be that superfluous Ca2+ would lead to the coalescence of mass emulsion droplets and bring about oil-phase releasing, so as to the release of CUR. The above results indicated that the ALG-CDs@Ca2+ (0.04 M) stabilized Pickering emulsions had the potential of encapsulate hydrophobicity drug.

The percentage of total CUR content in mixture after each digestion processing to theoretically added CUR content was considered as CUR retention rate, which was showed in Fig. 6C. The initial retained CUR in Pickering emulsion was 100%. The retained CUR for all Pickering emulsions after digestion of oral and SGF was kept a relatively high amount, nearly 80%–97%, indicating that CUR was released with a very slow rate at oral and SGF phase. This result was due to the forming of “egg-box” structure that ALG cross-linked with divalent cations Ca2+. The mean retention rate was about 38.44% and 82.55% with the addition of 0 M and 0.04 M Ca2+, respectively. Compared with Pickering emulsion without Ca2+, the retention rate of CUR during the whole digestion process was higher with more Ca2+ added to ALG. From the above results, Pickering emulsions had the ability to improve the stability and control the release of lipophilic compounds in gastrointestinal tract.. Therefore, the stronger the cross-link, the harder it is to release the CUR that encapsulated in Pickering emulsion stabilized by ALG-CDs@Ca2+ (0.04 M), finally, achieving the purpose of sustained release for hydrophobic drug.

Next, we aimed to test the cytotoxic effect of the Pickering emulsion and the capability of Pickering emulsion to maintain CUR activity by conducing the cytotoxicity assay on the various formulations non-loaded and loaded with CUR. Fig. 7 A showed the survival of Caco-2 cells after 24 h of culture with different concentration of ACPE, ACPEC and PC. It can be seen that the higher the concentration of CUR, the lower the cell survival rate, while ACPE had no toxic effect on Caco-2 cells. It is showed that the cell viability of ACPEC remained above 80% after incubation for 24 h at the CUR concentration of 5∼25 μg/mL, while that of PC was below 75% at the concentration of 15∼25 μg/mL.. The results clearly suggested that ACPE had good biocompatibility to Caco-2 cells and ACPEC had a slow releasing effect on the release of CUR. Fig. 7B showed the survival status of Caco-2 cells for 48 h with the addition of different concentration of ACPE, ACPEC and PC, which also showed that the higher the concentration of CUR inhibited the cell survival rate. As can be seen from Fig. 7B, although the survival rate of Caco-2 in the ACPE group decreased compared with that in culture for 24 h, it was still higher than 80%. However, when the emulsion concentration was too high, the average survival rate of Caco-2 was 60.92%. When the drug concentration reached up to 20 μg/mL, the average survival rate of cells in the ACPEC group was 66.95%, while the average survival rate for PC group was 34.52%. When the drug concentration reached to 25 μg/mL, the average survival rate of cells in the ACPEC group was 51.25%, while it was 2.45% in the PC group. It is known that the toxicity of nanomaterial is generally regulated by the surface physicochemical modification and physiological environment (Alexis, Pridgen, Molnar, & Farokhzad, 2008). In this study, all the results showed that the ACPE was basically harmless to the Caco-2 cells and had good biocompatibility, what's more, ACPEC had better sustained drug release ability.

Since the prepared Pickering emulsion has the function of fluorescence imaging, it can be observed whether the Pickering emulsion or drug-loading emulsion can be taken up by cells through automatic living cell imaging system without staining. Therefore, the cellular uptake of Pickering emulsion non-loaded and loaded with CUR (20 μg/mL) was evaluated by Caco-2 cells after incubating for 6 h using automatic living cell imaging system. Fig. 7C showed the cell imaging of Caco-2 cells incubated with ACPE and Fig. 7D showed the cell imaging of Caco-2 cells incubated with ACPEC. In Fig. 7C (c1) and D (d1), the Caco-2 cells clusters had smooth borders and the clones could be seen in the bright field. Moreover, the cells still grew tightly against the wall, and the shape of the cells did not change, indicating that the cells were growing well.. Fig. 7C (c2, c3) and Fig. 7D (d2, d3) showed the cell morphology under excitation sources 405 nm and 488 nm. The cells exhibited green and blue coloring in the cytoplasm region, suggesting that the ALG-CDs@Ca2+ had a distribution in the entire cell cytoplasm. However, cells incubated with CUR-loaded Pickering emulsions showed a darker blue and green color. This may be the influence of CUR that have fluorescence characteristic, thus indicating that either the Pickering emulsion or the CUR-loaded Pickering emulsion can be taken up by the Caco-2 cells.

In conclusion, an amphiphilic colloidal particle ALG-CDs with fluorescent properties was used to co-stabilize Pickering emulsions with appropriate amounts of Ca2+. The particle size of Pickering emulsions became smaller and more uniform with increasing of Ca2+ concentration, however, it occurred demulsification when the concentration was too large (0.08 M). Therefore, the addition of Ca2+ can influence the stability of the Pickering emulsion, the particle size and the thickness of the interfacial film, which can be visualized by CLSM in situ.

Due to the excellent fluorescence characteristics of ALG-CDs aggregates, visualization can be achieved not only at the oil-water interface but also during in vitro digestion. Furthermore, the Pickering emulsion transformed into emulsion gel with the proton-triggered and ionic interaction. In addition, the release of FFA was inhibited and the retention rate of drug was high by the combination effect of interfacial film of Pickering emulsions, which can be used for drug sustained release.

ACPE and ACPEC incubated Caco-2 cell lines for 24 h and 48 h in vitro cell viability test results showed that ACPE had good biocompatibility with Caco-2 cells and ACPEC had better drug sustained release ability. The fluorescence image of Caco-2 cells indicates that the Pickering emulsion or the CUR-loaded Pickering emulsion can be taken up by the Caco-2 cells.

Overall, due to its good biocompatibility, excellent fluorescence and gelation in the gastrointestinal tract, the prepared Pickering emulsion have potential application in oral administration and sustained drug release, as well as visualization of emulsion digestion and cell uptake in situ.
The work described has not been submitted elsewhere for publication, in whole or in part, and all the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.

Moreover, we thank Ye Li, from institute of environment and plant protection, Chinese academy of tropical agricultural sciences, for CLSM experiments help.
